title: An Introduction to Brutalism: The Iconic Postwar Architectural Style That Combined Utopianism and Concrete
description:
in Architecture, History |   	March 26th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The artiôÙfiôÙcial lanôÙguage of EsperanôÙto was conôÙceived with high ideals i
body:
in Architecture, History |   	March 26th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The artiôÙfiôÙcial lanôÙguage of EsperanôÙto was conôÙceived with high ideals in mind. In the eighôÙteen-eightôÙies, its creôÙator L. L. ZamenôÙhof enviôÙsioned it as the uniôÙverôÙsal secôÙond lanôÙguage of humanôÙiôÙty, and if it hasôÙnãt achieved that staôÙtus by now, it at least remains the worldãs most wideôÙly spoôÙken conôÙstructôÙed auxôÙilôÙiary lanôÙguage. One facôÙtor comôÙpliôÙcatôÙing its spread is that no lanôÙguage, even one guidôÙed by interôÙnaôÙtionôÙalôÙism, can remain the same for long enough in two difôÙferôÙent culôÙtures. As in spoôÙken and writôÙten lanôÙguages, so in the conôÙcrete one of archiôÙtecôÙture ã and in the case of the style known as BruôÙtalôÙism, that would be litôÙerôÙal conôÙcrete. Meant to make humanôÙiôÙty whole again after the SecôÙond World War, its buildôÙings endôÙed up being rather more parôÙticôÙuôÙlar, and less utopiôÙan, than their archiôÙtects intendôÙed.
ExamôÙples aplenôÙty appear in the new video above from Built NarôÙraôÙtive, which offers what amounts to a postôÙcard tour of BruôÙtalôÙist (and BruôÙtalôÙism-adjaôÙcent) buildôÙings from around the world. Named for its main mateôÙrôÙiôÙal bûˋton brut, or raw conôÙcrete, the style came into its own durôÙing the rebuildôÙing of war-ruined secôÙtions of British and conôÙtiôÙnenôÙtal EuroôÙpean cities ã and, over in the U.S., the rapid proôÙlifôÙerôÙaôÙtion and expanôÙsion of colôÙlege camôÙpusôÙes ã which had to be done quickôÙly and under less-than-extravôÙaôÙgant budôÙgets.
Libraries, research facilôÙiôÙties, city halls, adminôÙisôÙtraôÙtive buildôÙings, courôÙtôÙhousôÙes, housôÙing projects: these are the sorts of strucôÙtures that most often took BruôÙtalôÙist form in the nineôÙteen-fifties, sixôÙties, and sevôÙenôÙties, resultôÙing in the instiôÙtuôÙtionôÙal, bureauôÙcratôÙic, and in some places totalôÙiôÙtarôÙiôÙan assoôÙciôÙaôÙtions it still has today.
Some pubôÙlicly loathed BruôÙtalôÙist buildôÙings, like the TriôÙcorn CenôÙtre in Portsmouth and theô Third Church of Christ, SciôÙenôÙtist in WashôÙingôÙton, D.C. have been torn down, often after decades of negôÙliôÙgent mainôÙteôÙnance. OthôÙers, like the BarôÙbiôÙcan Estate in LonôÙdon or HabiôÙtat 67 in MonôÙtreôÙal, are now beloved sites of pilôÙgrimôÙage. WideôÙly acknowlôÙedged masôÙters of BruôÙtalôÙism include Le CorôÙbusier,ô who pioôÙneered it with buildôÙings like the Unitûˋ dãHabiôÙtaôÙtion in MarôÙseille (not Berlin, conôÙtra the capôÙtion in the video) and KenôÙzo Tange (proôÙnounced ãtawn-gay,ã not ãtangã as the narôÙraôÙtor says it), whose work steered the JapanôÙese verôÙsion of the moveôÙment in its own subôÙtle, someôÙtimes playôÙful direcôÙtions. Now, thanks in part to the rapid difôÙfuôÙsion of archiôÙtecôÙturôÙal phoôÙtogôÙraôÙphy made posôÙsiôÙble by social media, a new enthuôÙsiôÙast of BruôÙtalôÙism seems to be born every minute. Even if they donãt believe thatô archiôÙtecôÙture can bring a new world into being, they still feel the pull of aô future that nevôÙer came ã or, at any rate, hasôÙnãt come yet.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
EveryôÙthing You Ever WantôÙed to Know About the BeauôÙty of BruôÙtalôÙist ArchiôÙtecôÙture: An IntroôÙducôÙtion in Six Videos
Why PeoôÙple Hate BruôÙtalôÙist BuildôÙings on AmerôÙiôÙcan ColôÙlege CamôÙpusôÙes
Why Do PeoôÙple Hate ModôÙern ArchiôÙtecôÙture?: A Video Essay
GoodôÙbye to the NakôÙaôÙgin CapôÙsule TowôÙer, Tokyoãs Strangest and Most UtopiôÙan ApartôÙment BuildôÙing
The World AccordôÙing to Le CorôÙbusier: An AniôÙmatôÙed IntroôÙducôÙtion to the Most ModôÙern of All ArchiôÙtects
An EspresôÙso MakôÙer Made in Le Corbusierãs BruôÙtalôÙist ArchiôÙtecôÙturôÙal Style: Raw ConôÙcrete on the OutôÙside, High-End Parts on the Inside
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Meet the ãTelharmonium,ã the First Synthesizer (and Predecessor to Muzak), Invented in 1897
description: Before the New Year, we brought you footage of RussôÙian polyôÙmathôÙic invenôÙtor Lûˋon Theremin demonôÙstratôÙing the strange instruôÙment that bea
body:
Before the New Year, we brought you footage of RussôÙian polyôÙmathôÙic invenôÙtor Lûˋon Theremin demonôÙstratôÙing the strange instruôÙment that bears his surôÙname, and we notôÙed that the Theremin was the first elecôÙtronôÙic instruôÙment. This is not strictôÙly true, though it is the first elecôÙtronôÙic instruôÙment to be mass proôÙduced and wideôÙly used in origôÙiôÙnal comôÙpoôÙsiôÙtion and perôÙforôÙmance. But like bioôÙlogôÙiôÙcal evoôÙluôÙtion, the hisôÙtoôÙry of musiôÙcal instruôÙment develôÙopôÙment is litôÙtered with dead ends, anomôÙalies, and forôÙgotôÙten ancesôÙtors (such as the octoôÙbass). One such obscure oddôÙiôÙty, the TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum, appeared almost 20 years before the Theremin, and it was patentôÙed by its AmerôÙiôÙcan invenôÙtor, ThadôÙdeus Cahill, even earôÙliôÙer, in 1897. (See some of the many diaôÙgrams from the origôÙiôÙnal patent below.)

Cahill, a lawyer who had preôÙviôÙousôÙly inventôÙed devices for pianos and typeôÙwritôÙers, creôÙatôÙed the Telharmoniumãalso called the Dynamaphoneãto broadôÙcast music over the teleôÙphone, makôÙing it a preôÙcurôÙsor not to the Theremin but to the latôÙer scourge of teleôÙphone hold music. ãIn a large way,ã writes Jay WillisôÙton at Synthmuseum.com, ãCahill inventôÙed what we know of today as ãMuzak.ãã
He built the first proôÙtoôÙtype TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum, the Mark I, in 1901. It weighed sevôÙen tons. The final incarôÙnaôÙtion of the instruôÙment, the Mark III, took 50 peoôÙple to build at the cost of $200,000 and was ã60 feet long, weighed almost 200 tons and incorôÙpoôÙratôÙed over 2000 elecôÙtric switchôÙesãÎ. Music was usuôÙalôÙly played by two peoôÙple (4 hands) and conôÙsistôÙed of mostôÙly clasôÙsiôÙcal works by Bach, Chopin, Greig, RossiôÙni and othôÙers.ã The workôÙings of the garôÙganôÙtuôÙan machine resemôÙble the boilôÙer room of an indusôÙtriôÙal facilôÙiôÙty. (See sevôÙerôÙal phoôÙtographs here.)

NeedôÙless to say, this was a highôÙly impracôÙtiôÙcal instruôÙment. NevôÙerôÙtheôÙless, Cahill not only found willôÙing investors for the enorôÙmous conôÙtrapôÙtion, but he also staged sucôÙcessôÙful demonôÙstraôÙtions in BalôÙtiôÙmore, thenãafter disôÙasôÙsemôÙbling and movôÙing the thing by trainãin New York. By 1905, his New EngôÙland ElecôÙtric Music ComôÙpaôÙny ãmade a deal with the New York TeleôÙphone ComôÙpaôÙny to lay speôÙcial lines so that he could transôÙmit the sigôÙnals from the TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum throughôÙout the city.ã Cahill used the term ãsynôÙtheôÙsizôÙingã in his patent, which some say makes the TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum the first synôÙtheôÙsizôÙer, though its operôÙaôÙtion was as much mechanôÙiôÙcal as elecôÙtronôÙic, using a comôÙpliôÙcatôÙed series of gears and cylinôÙders to repliôÙcate the musiôÙcal range of a piano. (See the operôÙaôÙtion explained in the video at the top.) ãRaised bumps on cylinôÙders helped creôÙate musiôÙcal conôÙtour notes,ã writes PopôÙuôÙlar MechanôÙics, ãnot unlike a music box, with the size of the cylinôÙder deterôÙminôÙing the pitch.ã

The huge, very loud TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum Mark III endôÙed up in the baseôÙment of the MetôÙroôÙpolôÙiôÙtan Opera House for a time as Cahill worked on his scheme for pumpôÙing music through the teleôÙphone lines. But this planô did not come off smoothôÙly. ãThe probôÙlem was,ã PopôÙuôÙlar MechanôÙics points out,ã all cables leak off radio waves. SendôÙing a giganôÙtic, ampliôÙfied sigôÙnal on turn-of-the-20th-cenôÙtuôÙry phone lines was bound to cause trouôÙble.ã The TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum creôÙatôÙedô interôÙferôÙence on othôÙer phone lines and even interôÙruptôÙed Naval radio transôÙmisôÙsions. ãRumor has it,ã the DouôÙglas AnderôÙson School of the Arts writes, ãthat a New York busiôÙnessôÙman, infuôÙriôÙatôÙed by the conôÙstant netôÙwork interôÙferôÙence, broke into the buildôÙing where the TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum was housed and destroyed it, throwôÙing pieces of the machinôÙery into the HudôÙson rivôÙer below.ã
The stoôÙry seems unlikeôÙly,ô but itô serves as a symôÙbol for the instruôÙmenôÙtãsô colôÙlapse. Cahillãs comôÙpaôÙny foldôÙed in 1908, though the final TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum supôÙposôÙedôÙly remained operôÙaôÙtional until 1916. No recordôÙings of the instruôÙment have surôÙvived, and ThadôÙdeus Cahillãs brothôÙer Arthur evenôÙtuôÙalôÙly sold the last proôÙtoôÙtype off for scrap in 1950 after failôÙing to find a buyôÙer. The entire ratioôÙnale for the instruôÙment had beenô supôÙplantôÙed by radio broadôÙcastôÙing. The TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum may have failed to catch on, but it still had a sigôÙnifôÙiôÙcant impact. Its unique design inspired anothôÙer imporôÙtant elecôÙtronôÙic instruôÙment, the HamôÙmond organ. And its very exisôÙtence gave musiôÙcal futurôÙists a vision. The DouôÙglas AnderôÙson School writes:
Despite its final demise, the TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum trigôÙgered the birth of elecôÙtronôÙic musicãThe ItalôÙian ComôÙposôÙer and intelôÙlecôÙtuôÙal FerôÙrucôÙcio Busoni inspired by the machine at the height of its popôÙuôÙlarôÙiôÙty was moved to write his ãSketch of a New AesôÙthetôÙic of Musicã (1907) which in turn became the clarôÙiôÙon call and inspiôÙraôÙtion for the new genôÙerôÙaôÙtion of elecôÙtronôÙic comôÙposers such as Edgard Varû´se and LuiôÙgi RusôÙsoôÙlo.
The instruôÙment also made quite an impresôÙsion on anothôÙer AmerôÙiôÙcan invenôÙtor, Mark Twain, who enthuôÙsiôÙasôÙtiôÙcalôÙlyô demonôÙstratôÙed it through the teleôÙphoneô durôÙingô a New Yearãs gathôÙerôÙing at his home, after givôÙing a speech about his own not inconôÙsidôÙerôÙable staôÙtus as an innoôÙvaôÙtor and earôÙly adopter of new techôÙnoloôÙgies. ãUnforôÙtuôÙnateôÙly for ThadôÙdeus Cahill,ã writes William Weir atô The HartôÙford Courant, ãTwainãs supôÙport wasôÙnãt enough to make a sucôÙcess of the TelôÙharôÙmoôÙniôÙum.ã Learn more about the instruôÙmenôÙtãs hisôÙtoôÙry from thisô book.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2016.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
SoviôÙet InvenôÙtor Lûˋon Theremin Shows Off the Theremin, the EarôÙly ElecôÙtronôÙic InstruôÙment That Could Be Played WithôÙout Being Touched (1954)
The HisôÙtoôÙry of ElecôÙtronôÙic Music, 1800ã2015: Free Web Project CatôÙaôÙlogues the Theremin, Fairlight & OthôÙer InstruôÙments That RevôÙoôÙluôÙtionôÙized Music
The FasôÙciôÙnatôÙing StoôÙry of How the ElecôÙtric Music PioôÙneer Delia DerôÙbyshire CreôÙatôÙed the OrigôÙiôÙnalô DocôÙtor Whoô Theme (1963)
Hear SevôÙen Hours of Women MakôÙing ElecôÙtronôÙic Music (1938ã2014)
Thomas DolôÙby Explains How a SynôÙtheôÙsizôÙer Works on a Jim HenôÙson Kids Show (1989)
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: When Soviet Youth Bootlegged Western Rock Music on Discarded XãRays: Hear Original Audio Samples
description:
in Film, History, Music |   	March 27th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
A catchy tribôÙute to mid-cenôÙtuôÙry SoviôÙet hipôÙsters popped up a few years back
body:
in Film, History, Music |   	March 27th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
A catchy tribôÙute to mid-cenôÙtuôÙry SoviôÙet hipôÙsters popped up a few years back in a song called ãStilyaôÙgiã by lo-fi L.A. hipôÙsters Puro Instinct. The lyrics tell of a charisôÙmatôÙic dude who impressôÙes ãall the girls in the neighôÙborôÙhoodã with his ãmagôÙniôÙtizôÙdatã and guiôÙtar. Wait, his what? His magôÙniôÙtizôÙdat, man! Like samizôÙdat, or underôÙground press, magôÙniôÙtizôÙdatãfrom the words for ãtape recorderã and ãpublishingããkept SoviôÙet youth in the know with surôÙrepôÙtiôÙtious recordôÙings of pop music. StilyaôÙgi (a post-war subôÙculôÙture that copied its style from HolôÙlyôÙwood movies and AmerôÙiôÙcan jazz and rock and roll) made and disôÙtribôÙuted conôÙtraôÙband music in the SoviôÙet Union. But, as an NPR piece informs us, ãbefore the availôÙabilôÙiôÙty of the tape recorder and durôÙing the 1950s, when vinyl was scarce, ingeôÙnious RusôÙsians began recordôÙing banned bootôÙleg jazz, booôÙgie wooôÙgie and rock ãnã roll on exposed Xãray film salôÙvaged from hosôÙpiôÙtal waste bins and archives.ã See one such Xãray ãrecordã above, and see here the fasôÙciôÙnatôÙing process draôÙmaôÙtized in the first scene of a 2008 RussôÙian musiôÙcal titled, of course, StilyaôÙgi (transôÙlatôÙed into EngôÙlish as ãHipstersããthe word litôÙerôÙalôÙly means ãobsessed with fashôÙionã).


	

These records were called roentôÙgôÙeôÙnizôÙdat (Xãray press) or, says Sergei Khrushchev (son of NikiôÙta), ãbone music.ã Author Anya von Bremzen describes them as ãforôÙbidôÙden WestôÙern music capôÙtured on the inteôÙriôÙors of SoviôÙet citôÙiôÙzensã: ãThey would cut the Xãray into a crude cirôÙcle with manôÙiôÙcure scisôÙsors and use a cigôÙaôÙrette to burn a hole. Youãd have Elvis on the lungs, Duke EllingôÙton on Aunt Mashaãs brain scanãÎ.ã The ghoulôÙish makeshift discs sure look cool enough, but what did they sound like? Well, as you can hear below in the BeaôÙtôÙles samôÙples, a bit like old VicôÙtroôÙla phonoôÙgraph records played through tiny tranôÙsisôÙtor radios on a squonky AM freôÙquenôÙcy.


	

Dressed in fashôÙions copied from jazz and rockôÙaôÙbilôÙly albums, stilyaôÙgi learned to dance at underôÙground nightôÙclubs to these tinôÙny ghosts of WestôÙern pop songs, and fought off the Komsomolãsuper-square LeninôÙist youth brigadesãwho broke up roentôÙgôÙeôÙnizôÙdat rings and tried to supôÙpress the influôÙence of bourôÙgeois WestôÙern pop culôÙture. AccordôÙing to ArteôÙmy TroitôÙsky, author of Back in the USSR: The True StoôÙry of Rock in RusôÙsia, these records were also called ãribsã: ãThe qualôÙiôÙty was awful, but the price was lowãa rouôÙble or rouôÙble and a half. Often these records held surôÙprisôÙes for the buyôÙer. Letãs say, a few secôÙonds of AmerôÙiôÙcan rock ãnã roll, then a mockôÙing voice in RussôÙian askôÙing: ãSo, thought youãd take a lisôÙten to the latôÙest sounds, eh?, folôÙlowed by a few choice epiôÙthets addressed to fans of stylôÙish rhythms, then silence.ã


	

See more images of bone music records over at LaughôÙing Squid and Wired co-founder Kevin KelôÙlyãsô blog Street Use, and above dig some hisôÙtorôÙiôÙcal footage of stilyaôÙgi jitôÙterôÙbugôÙging through what appears to be a kind of SoviôÙet trainôÙing film about WestôÙern influôÙence on SoviôÙet youth culôÙture, proôÙduced no doubt durôÙing the Khrushchev thaw when, as RussôÙian writer Vladimir Voinovich tells NPR, things got ãa litôÙtle more libôÙerôÙal than before.ã

Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2014.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
How to Spot a ComôÙmuôÙnistô by Using LitôÙerôÙary CritôÙiôÙcism: A 1955 ManôÙuôÙal from the U.S. MilôÙiôÙtary
Louis ArmôÙstrong Plays HisôÙtoric Cold War ConôÙcerts in East Berlin & Budapest (1965)
Read the CIAãsô SimôÙple SabôÙoôÙtage Field ManôÙuôÙal: A TimeôÙless Guide to SubôÙvertôÙing Any OrgaôÙniôÙzaôÙtion with ãPurôÙposeôÙful StuôÙpidôÙiôÙtyã (1944)
Bertolt Brecht TesôÙtiôÙfies Before the House Un-AmerôÙiôÙcan ActivôÙiôÙties ComôÙmitôÙtee (1947)
How the CIA SecretôÙly FundôÙed Abstract ExpresôÙsionôÙism DurôÙing the Cold War
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: A Short Film Joins the Timeless Swiss Masked Tradition of Silvesterchlausen
description: In communities throughout Switzerlandãs Appenzell Hinterland and Midland regions, a unique tradition with enigmatic origins unfolds around the New
body:
In communities throughout Switzerlandãs Appenzell Hinterland and Midland regions, a unique tradition with enigmatic origins unfolds around the New Year. Known as Silvesterchlausen, the custom entails a group of boys and men who don remarkable, handmade costumes with masks and headdresses that represent rural, wild, and natural scenes.
ãSilvesterchlausen,ã a dreamy short film by writer and director Andrew Norman Wilson, highlights this regional seasonal event, which occurs on December 31 and January 13. The first date marks the turn of the new year on the Gregorian calendar, while January 13 denotes the same on the Julian calendar. The ornately dressed mummers, in groups of six, polyphonically yodel and ring bells. ãThe ritual has been performed for at least 500 years, but nobody knows how or why it began,ã Wilson says.



Some of the performersã headwear resembles miniature parade floats, while otherworldly designs made from pinecones, mosses, grasses, and other organic items make some of them appear as though they have emerged directly from the earth. In small, tight-knit municipalities, the tradition is a rare instance of relative anonymity, as familiar residents disappear behind meticulously crafted garments.
The performers, known as ChlûÊuse, practice diligently for a month or so before the event, creating something of a ãChlûÊus fever.ã Boys form the groups and ãcontinue throughout their lives until the members are too old to withstand the physical toll of the 18-hour days,ã Wilson says, sharing that the participants build significant bonds.
As New Yearãs Eve arrives, the mummers connect houses with a red string, literally and figuratively stitching connections within the community. Then, as the ChlûÊuse move through villages and visit homes, local residents provide mulled wine to keep their bodies warm and spirits high.
See the film on Vimeo, and find more of Wilsonãs work on Instagram. If youãre in the Upper Midwest, you can experience a taste of this annual tradition in New Glarus, Wisconsin. You might also enjoy Ashley Suszczynskiãs incredible and mysterious photographs exploring European masking rituals.

schema.headline: A Short Film Joins the Timeless Swiss Masked Tradition of Silvesterchlausen

title: David Morrison's Alluring Drawings Spring from the Blank Page
description: David Morrison continues his hyperrealistic explorations of flowers, seeds, and plants, capturing the intricacies and alluring textures found thro
body:
David Morrison continues his hyperrealistic explorations of flowers, seeds, and plants, capturing the intricacies and alluring textures found throughout nature in lush colored pencil. Delicate, fine lines and smooth gradients prevail in the artistãs drawings, which present the organic subject matter as if it were bathed in light. Rendered in a soft haze, shadows of individual fronds and nodes add a deceptive sense of depth to the two-dimensional works.
The pieces shown here are some of Morrisonãs latest, and you can find more on his Instagram and via Garvey | Simon, where heãs represented.
ãBotanical Series No.4 Drawingã (2025), colored pencil, 29 x 15 inches 
ãBotanical No.3 Drawingã (2025), colored pencil, 29 x 15 inches
ãIceland Poppy,ã colored pencil on paper, 18 x 16.5 inches
Detail of ãBotanical Series No.4 Drawingã
Detail of ãIceland Poppyã
ãChinese Lantern No. 2ã (2025), colored pencil on paper, 18 x 16.5 inches

title: Ambiguity Reigns in Olaf Hajek's Mysterious Illustrations
description: For Olaf Hajek, difference isnãt about opposition but rather about identifying connections. The Berlin-based illustrator renders dense, uncanny co
body:
For Olaf Hajek, difference isnãt about opposition but rather about identifying connections. The Berlin-based illustrator renders dense, uncanny compositions that nod to Surrealist icons like Leonora Carrington and Remedios Varo. Nature and culture entwine, and magic and mystery veil each scene. These dreamlike moments of intrigue ask the viewer to suspend preconceived notions and instead, enjoy the allure of the ambiguous.
Hajek is an avid traveler and cultural consumer, offering him a vast repository of images from a variety of sources and locales. Folklore, vernacular traditions, spiritual practices, and natural motifs blend into a distinguishable aesthetic. ãWhat interests me is not so much their differences, but the connections between themãthe possibility of developing a universal visual language by bringing diverse influences together. This blending becomes a way of transcending cultural boundaries and revealing something shared and timeless,ã he says. 

Ambiguity is a central point of Hajekãs practice, emerging technically through superimposed florals and figures, dramatic shifts in scale, and a tension between decay and renewal. Gender and conceptions of masculinity, in particular, are depicted with a sense of softness and fluidity, particularly through symbolic, botanical motifs in vibrant color.
Hajek works in parallel, if not complementary, practices, sketching and painting on paper in a looser, more reflexive manner. ãI embrace the unexpectedãhow colors interact, how forms dissolve into one another, and how compositions evolve organically. Especially in works on paper, intuition plays a central role; they feel more immediate, almost like a direct dialogue with the moment,ã he tells Colossal. 
Shifting to the canvas, though, requires a clearer vision, and the two approaches are an essential pairing in his practice. ãThey are part of the same artistic process, which constantly moves between intention and surprise, between structure and freedom,ã he says.
Hajek is participating in several upcoming exhibitions, including a group show at Feinkunst Krû¥ger in Hamburg and two solo presentations at Museum Franz Xaver Stahl in Erding and Kaplan Projects in Palma de Mallorca. Until then, find more of his work on Instagram.

schema.headline: Ambiguity Reigns in Olaf Hajek's Mysterious Illustrations

title: Frank Relle's Photos Revel in Louisiana's Otherworldly Swampland
description: When photographer Frank Relle was nine years old, he remembers sneaking out of the house he grew up in in New Orleans just before daybreak to catc
body:
When photographer Frank Relle was nine years old, he remembers sneaking out of the house he grew up in in New Orleans just before daybreak to catch the sunriseãan event he found frustratingly difficult to explain to others, as much as he wished to share the experience. It was only years later that he discovered the camera, and he reflects on this time now through the lens of an excerpt from the essay ãBetween Yes and Noã by Albert Camus: ãA manãs work is nothing but this slow trek to rediscover, through the detours of art, those two or three great and simple images in whose presence his heart first opened.ã
Relle adds, ãThe swamp was that opening for me. I do not fully understand how. I went in once, and something happened; I changed, and then I kept going back.ã  The New Orleans-based photographer still returns to the swamps of Louisiana, watched over by bald cypress trees draped in ethereal swathes of Spanish moss. He canoes onto the calm waters, capturing the transition between day and night amid the sounds of birds and other creatures that make their homes there.
ãBabsooã
ãI work in the swamp because it returns me to a way of being that feels older, quieter, and more true,ã Relle tells Colossal, continuing:

Out there, surrounded by trees, insects, birds, reflections, and dark water, I stop living inside the noise of my own mind. The swamp pulls me out of the island of myself and places me back inside a larger living world. In that state, I feel wonder, connection, and a kind of freedom. Photography became my way of sharing that feelingãnot by explaining it but by inviting others into it.

Relleãs series Until the Water explores Louisianaãs otherworldly bayous through a lens of serene reverence. He places lights beneath boughs and trunks, illuminating trees against darkening horizons to emphasize their billowing shapes amid expansive wetlands distinctive to the Gulf Coast region of North America.
Time is both evident and seemingly suspended in Relleãs photos, as within the context of a single day ending or beginning, we observe mature cypresses that may have weathered hundreds of years. (The oldest known living tree in eastern North America is a bald cypress in North Carolina thatãs more than 2,600 years old.) Some of the trees are abundantly leafy and full, while others are bare, struggling, or cracked open.
ãLemeireã
ãThe swamp at two in the morning is not quiet; it is one of the loudest places I have ever been,ã Relle says. ãBut a photograph of it is silent. And in that silence, there is an opening. A thresholdãÎ.That is what I wanted when I was small, watching the sky change. Not to describe it. To bring someone else to the edge of it. To share it without words.ã
Find more on Relleãs Instagram, and purchase prints in his online shop. And if youãre in New Orleans, visit his brick-and-mortar gallery on Royal Street.

schema.headline: Frank Relle's Photos Revel in Louisiana's Otherworldly Swampland
